title: 10 best herbal teas for hypertension

remedy_properties: Glycyrrhizin: This compound helps to lower blood pressure by inhibiting the activity of enzymes that convert angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, allowing blood vessels to relax and improve circulation.

Flavonoids: The flavonoids present in licorice tea have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in blood vessels, thereby improving blood flow and lowering blood pressure.

Isomaltose: This type of sugar found in licorice tea can help to lower blood pressure by increasing the production of nitric oxide, a natural vasodilator that relaxes blood vessels and improves blood flow.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with hypertension because of its unique properties and bioactive compounds. The tea contains flavonoids and glycyrrhizin, which have been shown to have a vasodilatory effect, causing blood vessels to relax and widen. This reduction in blood pressure is also attributed to the tea's ability to reduce aldosterone levels, a hormone that regulates sodium and water retention. As a result, herbal Glycyrrhiza glabra teas may help alleviate symptoms of hypertension and promote overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_constituents: Licorice Saponins (Glycyrrhizin): Licorice saponins help with hypertension by inhibiting the activity of the enzyme 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, which converts cortisol into its active form, leading to a reduction in blood pressure.
Flavonoids (Quercetin): Flavonoids, such as quercetin, help with hypertension by relaxing blood vessels and reducing inflammation, leading to a decrease in blood pressure.
Coumarins (Scopoletin): Coumarins, such as scopoletin, help with hypertension by reducing vascular resistance and improving endothelial function, leading to a decrease in blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used due to their high content of glycyrrhizin, a compound that helps lower blood pressure.
Leaves: The leaves are used for their antioxidant properties, which can help reduce oxidative stress and alleviate hypertension symptoms.
Barks: The barks are used for their anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation and contribute to lowering blood pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 teaspoons of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots and rinse them under cold running water.
Steep the dried roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release their properties.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea to sweeten it to taste.
Serve the tea immediately and drink it while it's still warm to experience its benefits.

remedy_properties: Diuretic property: Gromwell tea increases urine production, which helps to reduce blood pressure by removing excess sodium and water from the body, thereby reducing the workload on the heart.

Antioxidant property: The antioxidant compounds present in gromwell tea help to protect against oxidative stress and inflammation, which are known contributing factors to hypertension, and also help to maintain healthy blood vessels.

Vasodilatory property: Gromwell tea contains compounds that can relax blood vessels, allowing them to dilate and improve blood flow, which helps to lower blood pressure by reducing resistance in the blood vessels.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lithospermum officinale, also known as gromwell, teas helps with hypertension because of its natural vasodilatory properties. The tea contains compounds that help to relax and widen blood vessels, allowing for improved blood flow and reduced blood pressure. The flavonoids and phenolic acids present in Lithospermum officinale may also help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, contributing to its potential benefits for individuals with hypertension. Regular consumption of Lithospermum officinale tea may aid in maintaining healthy blood pressure levels.
remedy_constituents: Lithospermic acid: A phenolic acid known for its vasodilatory properties, helping to lower blood pressure by relaxing blood vessel walls.
Iridoid glycosides: A class of compounds known for their hypotensive effects, helping to reduce blood pressure by modulating cardiovascular function.

Alkaloids (including lithospermine and iridoid glycoside alkaloids): Known for their ability to inhibit the sympathetic nervous system, helping to reduce blood pressure by decreasing the force of contraction of the heart.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are used to make teas due to their high content of alkaloids, which have been shown to have a hypotensive effect.
Roots: The roots contain saponins, which may help in reducing blood pressure.
Flowers: The flowers are used in teas due to their flavonoid content, which has been reported to possess vasodilatory properties, potentially lowering blood pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 grams of dried lithospermum officinale flowers from a trusted source.
Combine the dried flowers with 250 milliliters of boiling water in a large teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse fully.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Serve the tea immediately and enjoy within 30 minutes of preparation time.

remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Hawthorn tea contains flavonoids, which are powerful antioxidants that can help to relax blood vessels and improve blood flow, reducing blood pressure.
Amino Acids: The amino acids in hawthorn tea, such as proline and hydroxyproline, can help to strengthen and tone blood vessel walls, improving circulation and lowering blood pressure.

Phenolic Acids: Hawthorn tea also contains phenolic acids, which have anti-inflammatory and antithrombotic properties that can help to reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease by preventing platelet aggregation and improving blood flow.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Crataegus monogyna, also known as hawthorn, teas helps with hypertension because of its unique combination of bioactive compounds. The tea contains flavonoids, oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs), and other polyphenols that have been shown to exert a vasodilatory effect, which helps to relax blood vessels and improve blood flow. This action can lower blood pressure by reducing peripheral resistance and cardiac workload. Additionally, hawthorn's antioxidant properties help to protect against oxidative stress and inflammation, further contributing to its potential benefits in managing hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Quercetin: This flavonoid helps with hypertension by acting as a vasodilator, causing blood vessels to relax and improve blood flow, thus reducing blood pressure.

Flavonoids (specifically, oligonol and epicatechin gallate): These polyphenolic compounds help with hypertension by inhibiting the activity of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), a key component in the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system that regulates blood pressure, and also by improving endothelial function.

Epicatechin: This flavan-3-ol compound helps with hypertension by reducing the activity of ACE, similar to flavonoids, and also by increasing the production of nitric oxide, a potent vasodilator that helps relax blood vessels and improve blood flow.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are used to make teas for hypertension due to their high content of flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to have vasodilatory effects and help reduce blood pressure.

Fruits: Fruits are used to make teas for hypertension due to their high content of flavonoids, specifically quercetin and epicatechin, which have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects that can help lower blood pressure.

Buds: Buds are used to make teas for hypertension due to their high content of flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to have vasodilatory effects and help reduce blood pressure, as well as their antioxidant properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves and fruits from the tree in late summer or early autumn when they are ripe.
Dry the harvested leaves and fruits using a low-temperature dehydrator at 40c for 2 hours.
Steep 2-3 teaspoons of dried leaves and fruits in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to make tea.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids and enjoy.
Store the dried leaves and fruits in an airtight container for up to 6 months for later use.

remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Flavonoids present in Ceylon cinnamon tea have been shown to help reduce blood pressure by improving blood vessel function and increasing nitric oxide production, which can help relax blood vessels and improve circulation.

Cinnamic acid: Cinnamic acid, a compound found in Ceylon cinnamon, has been demonstrated to have antihypertensive effects by inhibiting the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor that can raise blood pressure.

Polyphenols: The polyphenol content in Ceylon cinnamon tea has been shown to help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which are often associated with hypertension. By reducing these factors, Ceylon cinnamon tea may help improve overall cardiovascular health.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cinnamomum verum, also known as Ceylon cinnamon, teas helps with hypertension because of its unique properties that aid in regulating blood pressure. The active compounds in Ceylon cinnamon, such as cinnamaldehyde, have been found to improve blood vessel function and reduce inflammation, which can contribute to high blood pressure. Additionally, Ceylon cinnamon has been shown to lower the levels of LDL (bad) cholesterol and triglycerides, further contributing to its potential benefits in managing hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Cinnamaldehyde: Acts as a natural vasodilator, helping to relax blood vessels and reduce blood pressure by blocking the synthesis of endothelin, a potent vasoconstrictor.

Coumarins (cinnamomin and cinnamaldehyde): Exhibits antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in blood vessels, contributing to lower blood pressure.

Flavonoids (kaempferol and quercetin): Possess vasodilatory and antioxidant properties, helping to relax blood vessels, reduce blood pressure, and protect against vascular damage caused by high blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are often used in herbal teas to help lower blood pressure due to their high content of flavonoids and essential oils.
Barks: The barks of Cinnamomum verum are traditionally used in teas to aid in reducing hypertension due to their cinnamon oil content, which has blood-thinning properties.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes are used in teas for their anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate blood pressure by reducing inflammation in blood vessels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 tablespoons of dried cinnamomum verum bark from a trusted supplier or local herbalist.
Grind the dried bark into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle for 2 minutes.
Combine 1 teaspoon of the ground cinnamomum verum powder with 1 cup of boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors and oils to infuse into the water.
Strain the tea and discard the solids before serving the hot tea in your favorite cup.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: The flavonoids, phenolic acids, and lignans present in Purple Coneflower tea have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in blood vessels, which is a common underlying factor in hypertension.

Antioxidant activity: The antioxidants in Purple Coneflower tea, including quercetin and luteolin, neutralize free radicals that can damage blood vessels and contribute to high blood pressure, helping to protect against oxidative stress.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea, also known as purple coneflower, teas helps with hypertension because of its unique composition of flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkylamides. remedy_recipe: Gather 20-30 grams of dried echinacea purpurea flowers or roots.

Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried echinacea purpurea in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove the solids.

Chop the clean leaves into small pieces using a sharp knife to release the essential oils.

Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried matricaria chamomilla flowers for every 8 ounces of boiling water.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Stinging nettle teas helps with hypertension because of its unique blend of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids, terpenoids, and saponins. Steep the mixture for 5-10 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chaste tree teas helps with hypertension because its active compound, casticin, has been shown to have a positive impact on blood pressure levels. remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried vitex agnus-castus leaves and stems in the morning after sunrise.
Combine 2 tablespoons of the dried herb or 4-6 teaspoons of the fresh herb with boiling water in a teapot.

remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried ginkgo biloba leaves for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the ginkgo biloba leaves in boiling water for seven to ten minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large cup.
Add one tablespoon of honey for every eight ounces of tea to taste and stir well.

Barks: Containing phenolic compounds, which exhibit vasodilatory effects, helping to widen blood vessels and alleviate hypertension symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh tilia cordata leaves and flowers from a trusted source or store dried form for later use.
Measure out 10-15 grams of tilia cordata leaves and flowers into a large bowl or tea infuser.
Heat filtered water to a boiling point and let it cool down for 5-7 minutes to reach a temperature between 80-90°c.
Pour the heated water over the tilia cordata mixture and allow it to steep for 10-15 minutes to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids, then consume immediately or store in refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Starfruit teas helps with hypertension because of its unique combination of antioxidants, flavonoids, and potassium. The antioxidants in starfruit tea help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which are often associated with high blood pressure. Flavonoids in the tea also help to improve blood vessel function and reduce blood pressure by relaxing blood vessels. Additionally, the potassium content in the tea helps to balance sodium levels in the body, further contributing to its hypotensive effects, making it a natural and effective remedy for managing hypertension.
remedy_properties: Potassium content: Starfruit tea contains potassium, a mineral that can help lower blood pressure by counteracting the effects of sodium and relaxing blood vessels.
Flavonoids and phenolic acids: The tea is rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to reduce hypertension by improving blood vessel function and decreasing inflammation.
Antioxidant activity: Starfruit tea's antioxidant properties may help protect against oxidative stress and inflammation in blood vessels, reducing the risk of hypertension.
remedy_parts: Roots: Starfruit roots are rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, which help to reduce blood pressure by relaxing blood vessels.
Rhyzomes: The rhizomes of the starfruit plant contain saponins, which have been shown to lower blood pressure and improve cardiovascular health.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried averrhoa carambola leaves in sufficient quantity for the desired batch size.
Rinse the harvested leaves thoroughly with clean water to remove dirt and impurities.
Boil 2 cups of filtered water in a large pot for 5 minutes to create a hot brewing medium.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried averrhoa carambola leaves or 2 tablespoons of fresh leaves in the boiling water for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and serve immediately after preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wood sorrel teas helps with hypertension because it contains a unique combination of flavonoids, saponins, and anthocyanins that work synergistically to relax blood vessels, reducing blood pressure levels. The diuretic properties of wood sorrel also help eliminate excess sodium and water from the body, further contributing to its blood pressure-lowering effects. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in blood vessel walls, which can contribute to hypertension. By regularly consuming wood sorrel teas, individuals may be able to naturally manage their blood pressure levels.
remedy_properties: High levels of Malic acid: Malic acid has been shown to have a blood pressure-lowering effect, which can help to alleviate symptoms of hypertension by reducing peripheral resistance and vasodilating blood vessels.

Potassium content: Wood Sorrel tea is a rich source of potassium, an essential mineral that helps to counterbalance the effects of sodium in the body and promote healthy blood pressure levels by regulating fluid balance and blood vessel function.

Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compounds: The antioxidants and polyphenols present in Wood Sorrel tea may help to reduce inflammation in the blood vessels, which can contribute to hypertension, and also protect against oxidative stress that can exacerbate high blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in flavonoids and alkaloids, which help to reduce blood pressure by relaxing blood vessels and improving circulation.
Barks: The barks contain saponins and phenolic compounds that work as a natural diuretic, helping to remove excess fluids from the body and lower blood pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh rumex acetosella leaves measuring 2 tablespoons or 15-20 grams for every cup of water.
Rinse the collected leaves gently with cold running water to remove dirt and debris.
Combine the cleaned leaves with 1 cup of boiling distilled water and allow steeping for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass container discarding solids.
Store the prepared tea in an airtight container refrigerated at 40°f to 45°f within 2 hours of preparation.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to hypertension that are treated with herbal teas are conditions such as hypotension, low blood pressure, and cardiovascular issues like angina and arrhythmia. Herbs like Ginkgo biloba, Hawthorn, and Ginger are used to improve circulation, reduce blood pressure, and prevent platelet aggregation. These teas can help alleviate symptoms of palpitations, dizziness, and shortness of breath associated with these conditions.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for hypertension are a natural remedy that helps to lower blood pressure by promoting relaxation, reducing stress, and improving cardiovascular health. The best herbal teas for hypertension include Lavandula angustifolia, Crataegus monogyna, Ginkgo biloba, Valeriana officinalis, Passiflora incarnata, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Hibiscus sabdariffa, Rauvolfia serpentina, Melissa officinalis, and Cynara scolymus. If left untreated, hypertension can lead to heart disease, stroke, and kidney damage, severely impacting one's quality of life, causing fatigue, shortness of breath, and decreased mobility, ultimately affecting daily routines and overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with hypertension is a blend of Hibiscus, Lavender, and Passionflower. Hibiscus tea has been shown to lower blood pressure by reducing sodium levels and relaxing blood vessels. Lavender tea calms the nervous system, reducing stress and anxiety that can contribute to hypertension. Meanwhile, Passionflower tea helps to regulate heart rate and blood pressure. Drinking a combination of these teas can promote relaxation, reduce blood pressure, and support overall cardiovascular health.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas helps with hypertension because of its unique combination of bioactive compounds. The tea contains linalool and linalyl acetate, which are known to have vasodilatory properties, allowing blood vessels to relax and widen. This reduction in blood pressure is a result of the tea's ability to decrease the production of angiotensin II, a hormone that constricts blood vessels. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties may also contribute to its blood pressure-lowering effects, providing a natural and potentially effective way to manage hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Linalool: This terpene has vasodilatory properties, which help to relax blood vessels and lower blood pressure.
Luteolin: This flavonoid acts as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, reducing the oxidative stress and inflammation associated with hypertension.
Apigenin: This flavonoid has a potential vasodilatory effect, which may help to lower blood pressure by improving blood flow and reducing vascular resistance.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used due to their calming and antihypertensive properties, which help in reducing blood pressure.
Flowers: Used because they are rich in flavonoids and other compounds that help in relaxing the blood vessels and reducing hypertension.
Stems: Used as they contain bioactive compounds that help in reducing inflammation and promoting cardiovascular health, which is beneficial for hypertension patients.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh lavandula angustifolia flowers in the early morning or late evening when they are at their peak fragrance.
Dry the harvested flowers in a single layer on a paper bag or a clean cloth for 7-10 days.
Grind 2 teaspoons of dried lavandula angustifolia flowers into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Steep 1 teaspoon of ground lavandula angustifolia powder in a cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup and serve immediately.

remedies.remedy_desc: Crataegus monogyna teas helps with hypertension because it contains flavonoids and polyphenols, which have been shown to have a positive effect on cardiovascular health. The flavonoids in Crataegus monogyna tea help to relax blood vessels, reducing blood pressure and improving circulation. Additionally, the antioxidants present in the tea help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, further contributing to its blood pressure-lowering effects. This makes Crataegus monogyna tea a promising natural remedy for managing hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: Flavonoids in Crataegus monogyna help with hypertension by improving blood vessel function, reducing inflammation, and inhibiting the production of endothelin-1, a potent vasoconstrictor.
Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids in Crataegus monogyna help with hypertension by inhibiting the activity of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), reducing blood pressure and promoting vasodilation.

Triterpenoids: Triterpenoids in Crataegus monogyna help with hypertension by exerting antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in blood vessels, and improving endothelial function.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to exhibit antioxidant and vasodilatory effects that help lower blood pressure.
Fruits: Contain various bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and triterpenoids, that may help regulate blood pressure and improve cardiovascular health.
Barks: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects that contribute to its potential in managing hypertension.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh leaves and twigs of crataegus monogyna in the early morning to ensure optimal potency.
Clean the harvested material thoroughly by rinsing it under cold running water to remove any dirt particles.
Dry the cleaned material in a well-ventilated area at a temperature below 30 degrees celsius for 1 week.
Steep 2-3 teaspoons of dried crataegus monogyna leaves in 250ml of boiling water for 5-10 minutes to release the active compounds.
Strain the tea and discard the solids to obtain a clear infusion that can be consumed immediately.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba teas helps with hypertension because it possesses natural compounds that promote vasodilation, allowing blood vessels to relax and expand, thereby reducing blood pressure. The flavonoids and terpenoids present in Ginkgo biloba tea help improve blood flow to the heart and brain, increasing oxygenation and reducing the risk of cardiovascular events. Regular consumption of Ginkgo biloba tea may also help to strengthen blood vessel walls, leading to a decrease in systolic and diastolic blood pressure, promoting overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: Help with hypertension by improving blood vessel function and reducing blood pressure through their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
Bilobalide: Helps with hypertension by inhibiting the release of neurotransmitters that cause blood vessels to constrict, thereby reducing blood pressure.
Ginkgolides: Assist in reducing hypertension by blocking the action of platelet-activating factor (PAF), a substance that causes blood vessels to constrict and blood pressure to rise.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are the most commonly used part of Ginkgo biloba in teas for hypertension because they contain flavonoids and terpenoids, which have vasodilatory properties that help lower blood pressure.

Seeds: The seeds are used in teas due to their potential to help regulate blood pressure by reducing inflammation and improving blood flow, although some studies suggest they may have adverse effects.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1-2 teaspoons of dried ginkgo biloba leaves and 1 cup of boiling water for the herbal tea.
Steep the ginkgo biloba leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the active ingredients.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Add honey or lemon to taste but be aware that sugar can mask the herbal flavor.
Let the tea cool to a comfortable temperature before drinking for optimal herbal benefits.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis teas helps with hypertension because it possesses a unique blend of compounds that promote relaxation and reduce stress levels, leading to lower blood pressure. The tea's active ingredients, including valerenic acid and valepotriates, have been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which in turn helps to slow down the heart rate and dilate blood vessels. This results in a decrease in blood pressure and an overall improvement in cardiovascular health. Regular consumption of Valeriana officinalis teas may therefore be beneficial for individuals with hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Isovaleryl Valerenate: This constituent helps with hypertension by exerting a sedative effect on the nervous system, reducing stress and anxiety that contribute to elevated blood pressure.
Valerenic Acid: Valerenic Acid helps with hypertension by inhibiting the activity of enzymes involved in the production of stress hormones, which can contribute to increased blood pressure.
Valeranone: This constituent helps with hypertension by reducing the sympathetic nervous system's activity, leading to a decrease in heart rate and blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Roots: Valerian roots are the most commonly used part due to their high concentration of valerenic acids, which have a calming effect on the nervous system and help regulate blood pressure.
Stems: Valerian stems contain a significant amount of valerenic acids, which contribute to their ability to reduce anxiety and hypertension by promoting relaxation and reducing stress.
Leaves: Valerian leaves contain smaller amounts of valerenic acids compared to the roots and stems, but they still contribute to the overall calming and relaxing effects of valerian tea on the body.
remedy_recipe: Harvest valeriana roots in late summer or early fall when the plant is in full bloom.
Dry valeriana roots in a well-ventilated area at 30c for 2 weeks to reduce moisture content.
Chop dried valeriana roots into small pieces using a sharp knife or mortar and pestle.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried valeriana root with 1 cup of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids immediately.

remedies.remedy_desc: Passiflora incarnata teas helps with hypertension because it contains flavonoids and other compounds that have a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing stress and anxiety, which are common contributors to high blood pressure. The tea's antispasmodic properties also help to relax blood vessels, improving circulation and lowering blood pressure. Additionally, Passiflora incarnata has been shown to inhibit the production of stress hormones, such as cortisol, which can contribute to hypertension. As a result, regular consumption of Passiflora incarnata tea may help to alleviate hypertension symptoms and promote overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: They help with hypertension by reducing vascular resistance and improving endothelial function, leading to a decrease in blood pressure.

Alkaloids (primarily Harmane and Harmine): They help with hypertension by exerting a direct action on the central nervous system, reducing sympathetic nervous activity, and promoting vasodilation, which in turn lowers blood pressure.

Saponins: They help with hypertension by inhibiting the reabsorption of sodium ions in the kidneys, reducing fluid retention and promoting diuresis, which ultimately leads to a decrease in blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used for their high content of flavonoids and alkaloids, which have a calming effect and help to lower blood pressure.
Fruits: Utilized for their rich antioxidant and flavonoid content, which contribute to the relaxation of blood vessels and reduction of blood pressure.
Roots: Employed for their high concentration of flavonoids and other bioactive compounds that help to reduce blood pressure and improve cardiovascular health.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 30-40 grams of fresh passiflora incarnata leaves and flowers in the morning when dew is still present.
Clean the harvested plant material under cold running water to remove dirt and debris thoroughly.
Dry the cleaned plant material in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried plant material in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool to room temperature before serving.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas helps with hypertension because it contains licorice root, which has been shown to have a vasodilatory effect, causing blood vessels to relax and widen. This leads to a decrease in blood pressure, reducing the strain on the heart and lowering the risk of cardiovascular disease. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce inflammation in blood vessels, further contributing to its blood pressure-lowering effects, promoting overall cardiovascular health and well-being.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: It helps with hypertension by blocking the enzyme 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, which converts cortisol into a more potent form that can increase blood pressure.
Licocellulose: It helps with hypertension by reducing the levels of cortisol and other steroid hormones that can contribute to high blood pressure.

Flavonoids (specifically, isoflavonoids and isoflavonoid glycosides): They help with hypertension by inhibiting the enzyme renin, which is involved in the renin-angiotensin system that regulates blood pressure, and also by improving blood vessel function and reducing oxidative stress.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Glycyrrhiza glabra contain glycyrrhizin, a compound that can help lower blood pressure by inhibiting the action of aldosterone, a hormone that can contribute to hypertension.
Leaves: The leaves of Glycyrrhiza glabra are rich in flavonoids and glycosides, which can help relax blood vessels and reduce blood pressure.

Fruits (not typically used in tea, but sometimes used in supplements or medicines): Fruits of Glycyrrhiza glabra contain glycyrrhizin, similar to the roots, which can help lower blood pressure by inhibiting the action of aldosterone.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots and store them in an airtight container.
Boil 1 liter of water in a pot for 5 minutes to prepare the hot water.
Steep the dried roots in the boiling water for 10 minutes to release the active ingredients.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve into a separate container to remove the solids.
Allow the tea to cool and then serve it in a cup after 10 minutes of preparation.

remedies.remedy_desc: Hibiscus sabdariffa teas helps with hypertension because it's rich in anthocyanins and other bioactive compounds that have potent vasodilatory effects. These compounds help relax blood vessels, reducing blood pressure and improving circulation. The tea has also been shown to inhibit the production of angiotensin-converting enzyme, a key player in blood pressure regulation. Additionally, its diuretic properties help eliminate excess fluids from the body, further contributing to its blood pressure-lowering effects, making it a popular natural remedy for managing hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Anthocyanins: These powerful antioxidants help reduce hypertension by relaxing blood vessels, improving blood flow, and reducing inflammation in the cardiovascular system.

Phenolic acids: These compounds have been shown to help lower blood pressure by inhibiting the production of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), a key factor in blood pressure regulation, and also by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the cardiovascular system.

Quercetin: This flavonoid has been found to help alleviate hypertension by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, improving endothelial function, and reducing vascular resistance, ultimately leading to a decrease in blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Flowers: The flowers of Hibiscus sabdariffa are the most commonly used part to make teas for hypertension due to their high content of anthocyanins and other bioactive compounds.
Leaves: The leaves are sometimes used to make teas for hypertension, particularly in traditional medicine, to take advantage of their antioxidant properties.

Fruits: The calyces of Hibiscus sabdariffa, which are the fleshy, leaf-like structures surrounding the flower, are sometimes referred to as the fruit and used to make teas for hypertension due to their potential to help lower blood pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 cup of dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers for a refreshing and caffeine-free herbal tea.
Steep the dried flowers in 8 oz of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the flavors.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a large cup to remove the flowers.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey or sugar to sweeten the tea to taste for a soothing drink.
Serve the herbal tea immediately and enjoy its vibrant red color and fruity flavor.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rauvolfia serpentina teas helps with hypertension because it contains compounds like reserpine and ajmaline, which have been shown to lower blood pressure by reducing peripheral resistance and renal vascular resistance. The tea's active ingredients also help to relax blood vessels and improve blood flow, allowing the heart to work more efficiently. Additionally, Rauvolfia serpentina has been found to have a diuretic effect, helping to remove excess fluid from the body and further reducing blood pressure. This makes it a popular natural remedy for hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Alkaloids: They help lower blood pressure by inhibiting the sympathetic nervous system, which reduces the heart rate and blood vessel constriction.
Serpentin: It has vasodilatory properties, which cause blood vessels to relax and widen, resulting in a decrease in blood pressure.
Raucaffecin: It has a hypotensive effect by inhibiting the release of neurotransmitters that stimulate the heart and blood vessels, leading to a decrease in blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Rauvolfia serpentina are used to make teas for hypertension due to their high content of alkaloids, particularly reserpine, which helps lower blood pressure.
Roots: The roots of Rauvolfia serpentina are used to make teas for hypertension due to their rich source of alkaloids, including ajmaline and serpentine, which aid in reducing blood pressure.
Stems: The stems of Rauvolfia serpentina are used to make teas for hypertension as they are another source of alkaloids, including serpentinine and yohimbine, which help regulate blood pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 30 to 60 grams of fresh rauvolfia serpentina roots and wash them thoroughly under running water for 5 minutes.
Chop the roots into small pieces and dry them in a low-temperature oven at 50 to 60 degrees celsius for 2 hours.
Grind 2 to 4 grams of dried rauvolfia serpentina root into a fine powder using a coffee grinder for 2 minutes.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the powder in 1 cup of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea and discard the solids to obtain the herbal rauvolfia serpentina tea for immediate consumption or storage.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis teas helps with hypertension because it contains compounds that promote relaxation and reduce stress levels. The herb's calming effects lower the body's fight-or-flight response, which in turn helps to decrease blood pressure. Additionally, Melissa officinalis has antioxidant properties that help to protect the cardiovascular system from damage caused by free radicals, promoting overall heart health. By reducing stress and promoting a healthy cardiovascular system, Melissa officinalis teas may be a useful natural remedy for managing hypertension. Its benefits can also promote a good night's sleep.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: It helps with hypertension by acting as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, which can reduce vascular resistance and promote vasodilation.
Menthol: It helps with hypertension by inducing vasodilation and reducing blood pressure through its effects on the nervous system and blood vessels.

Linalool: It helps with hypertension by exhibiting antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can reduce the formation of free radicals and decrease vascular resistance, ultimately leading to lowered blood pressure.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are the most commonly used part for teas due to their high concentration of essential oils, particularly linalool, which has a calming effect and helps lower blood pressure.
Stems: Stems contain a significant amount of flavonoids and phenolic acids, which are believed to help reduce hypertension by improving cardiovascular function and blood vessel elasticity.

Roots: Roots of Melissa officinalis are used in some tea blends due to their rich content of saponins and other bioactive compounds that may help lower blood pressure and prevent cardiovascular disease.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 to 2 ounces of fresh melissa leaves or 1/2 teaspoon of dried melissa flowers in the morning.
Dry the melissa leaves or flowers in a warm dark place for 1 to 2 hours to remove excess moisture.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried melissa flowers or 2 teaspoons of fresh melissa leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the melissa tea through a fine mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the melissa leaves or flowers.
Serve the melissa tea hot or iced after 10 to 15 minutes to allow the flavors to meld together.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cynara scolymus teas helps with hypertension because it is rich in flavonoids and saponins, which have been shown to have a diuretic effect, helping to reduce fluid retention and lower blood pressure. The tea also contains antioxidants that can help protect against cell damage and inflammation, both of which can contribute to high blood pressure. Additionally, Cynara scolymus has been found to have a positive impact on lipid profiles, reducing triglycerides and LDL cholesterol, further contributing to its potential benefits in managing hypertension.
remedy_constituents: Quercetin: A flavonoid that helps with hypertension by acting as an antioxidant and vasodilator, which helps to relax blood vessels and reduce blood pressure.
Cynarin: A sesquiterpene lactone that helps with hypertension by inhibiting the reabsorption of sodium and water in the kidneys, leading to a decrease in blood volume and pressure.
Naringenin: A flavonoid that helps with hypertension by inhibiting the angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), which reduces blood pressure and prevents the formation of vasoconstrictors.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help lower blood pressure.
Flowers: They are a good source of cardiotonic compounds, such as cynarin, which can help improve cardiovascular health and reduce hypertension.
Buds: They contain flavonoids and other bioactive compounds that have been shown to have vasodilatory effects, helping to relax blood vessels and lower blood pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 15-20 fresh cynara scolymus flowers on a sunny day when the buds are still tightly closed.
Rinse the harvested flowers in cold water to remove dirt and debris for 5 minutes.
Dry the rinsed flowers using a food dehydrator at 135 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release.
Strain the tea into a cup and serve warm or chilled immediately after preparation.
